Best way to understand how to use metrics is to actually look at some examples. It's so easy to go wrong. Everybody gets this wrong. I see it over and over again. So what you're seeing on my screen is this is a keyword level account where I've effectively eliminated all the keywords because I can't show that data. I was asked to review this account for a client. They manage this account themselves and they asked me to come in and just take a look and tell them what I see. So you can imagine to the left of where it says conversions, each line is a keyword phrase and with a different match type or whatever specific to this particular account. So let's go through some ways that we could evaluate this and what the data means and then this is going to illustrate why it's so hard to actually use metrics on their own. So let's just for the sake of argument say that we want to improve this account. That's what we've been asked to do. We are supposed to make this account better. So how would we do that? Well, let's just randomly start looking at places. We could look over at add relevance and we could say, okay, well, anything that's average or below average will just pause. Certainly one way we could do it. We could do the same thing with landing page experience and say, well, if it's a good average or below average, we don't really want and we don't have everything in here is above average for this account. So nothing to worry about there or we can move on the quality score and I the first to tell you the quality score and I've mentioned this in module three quality score is very important. So we can look here and we can think, well, we have sevens, eights, the lowest score is five. This seems great. So maybe we've got now we're starting to see something developing here. We have a below average ad relevance below or we have at the landing page is okay, but the quality score is low. Maybe this is one of what we should get rid of. And we could also look over it, start looking at costs. Wow, we spent a lot of money on this 14,000 for this search term. Do we really want to spend that much and look at or we could look at conversion rates and say, which has the lowest? We've got fours and ones, maybe we just pause everything lower than five. And by looking at these, we can look across any of these. We could, what's the last one here because click through rate I've mentioned as well is so important. So let's look at click through rate. Wow, some of these are extraordinary. These are small numbers, 33% obviously is extraordinary. All of these generally look good, but maybe we say because they're so good, we want to just pause everything that's lower than 10% because look how great these are. So we could optimize this and this is just one ad group, 10 or so keywords. We could optimize any of these things. If we really wanted to get sophisticated, we could move over and we could say, well, where are the conversions coming from? Well, we've got the most here. That's the one that costs so much though. So we have to pay attention to that. And some of these costs are really good. $147 costs for conversion versus $5700. So we can see there's a huge disparity. So maybe we just pause anything that's over a certain amount. Right? None of the things that I've mentioned so far mean anything. Nothing that I just suggested would be a rational way to manage this account or to optimize this account. Yeah, it is exactly what I see professional account managers do day after day. They come in, they look at these metrics and then they make decisions based on what is kept and what is paused or eliminated by the metrics within the account. There's one critical piece of information that you don't know by looking at this account. This client knows that their cost per conversion in order to be profitable needs to be $800 or less. Anything else on this page matters except for the cost per conversion relative to the client's target cost per conversion. So $938 cost per conversion is $138 more than they're willing to pay. Now should we pause this? That would be one where I would try to optimize this to within the $800 range. And we could talk about how we might do that. This one well within. This is an exceptional account. But look over here and see how many there are only 378 impressions, which means there are only 378 certain opportunities for this to appear. So search volume is very low. If you go all the way over here to search impression share, you can see in search impression share calculates the number of times the ad appeared relative to the number of times it was searched. So their ad appeared 93% of the time. So even though we would love to get way more traffic for this term, there just isn't more traffic to get there. They're getting it all. My sense in my memory is this is a branded term, so that's why it's exceptionally low cost per conversion. So it's an outlier. This one immediately never should have gotten this high. They never should have spent, they spent $5,500. It was going to be clear after spending $1,000 or $1,500 that this wasn't going to work. So they wasted $4,500 on this to get a $2,800 cost per conversion. That should be paused immediately. This one absolutely egregious. Someone spent $5,700 on a target CPA of $800. This is, it just went on and on and on and it should have been paused a long time ago. This one, well within the boundaries. This one over only one conversion, very low search volume, nothing else really matters. There's not a lot to optimize here because it's just not worth the time or energy. And then we get down to others where we don't have conversion data, but we have money that we've spent. This one we want to start paying attention to. We've already spent $1,000. We don't have any conversion data. I would not spend more than another $5,600 to pause this one. This one well within. This one over, but this one if you look at this one, I'm sorry, this is aggregated. So ignore that. But this actually brings us to a really interesting observation. If I just looked at this entire ad group, this line here is the total, which means this is the average cost per conversion. So if I answered that question for this client and they said, well, looking at that ad account, how is our, what's our average cost per conversion look like? I say, well, it's within 20% of your target. That sounds great. But what it doesn't show you is that there are lots of conversions at $147 and another conversion at $5,800. So this average is absolutely meaningless. We can't work with those averages because it doesn't convey any data that we can act on. We could have made countless decisions with this account about ad relevance or landing page or quality score, and we would have completely missed the obvious, which is that we have to understand this data in context. And the context is the context that matters more than anything else is that this client has a target cost per conversion of $800, meaning anything above $800 is questionable. And a farther away from $800, it gets the more questionable it is. So when we have $5,700 spent to get a single conversion, what we really see here is this should have been paused at $1,500. It had proved it wasn't going to be valuable. Who cares that it has a 10% click-through rate? Who cares that it has a reasonably good quality score of seven? Who cares that they're getting most of the search traffic? What matters is that this account is producing a customer for $5,700, and they can afford to pay $800. It's absolutely insane that this was left to continue like that. But then we can look at, for other opportunities, and we can see this one that we looked at here that we thought perhaps we should pause it because the ad relevance was below average. The quality score was the lowest out of all the quality scores, yet let's look over and see what happened. This produced five customers at $558, well within the $800 CPA. So this is a place where we might focus our attention to see if we can drive more traffic. Unfortunately, when we look over and we see that search impressions share is 92% already, we see there's a lot more, not a lot more there that we can do. So what I'm hoping to communicate with this very quick example is we can't look at any of these metrics in isolation. We have to look at every metric in service to the larger goals of the company. The goal for AdWords, for Google Ads, for this client is to produce customers at $800 or less. Nothing else matters if they're not producing customers for $800 or less. So anything that's wildly different than that, these two in particular, those need to be turned off. That you're just not going to optimize something from $5700 down to $800. It's just not possible. And the opportunity really isn't there. There's not a lot of traffic. You're not, it's very, very unlikely that you're going to optimize something for almost $2,800 cost per conversion down to $800. That's just not how it works. My guess is these two terms are either too general or they're not quite what the company is offering. It's not quite in line. It doesn't matter that they have high quality scores. Doesn't matter that the ad relevance is above average or that the landing page experience is above average. None of that matters because the only thing that matters is the cost per conversion is too high. So that then brings the question, well, if that's all that matters, why look at the rest of this? Why does it matter? Most of the time the answer is it doesn't. If all of this data for these two is irrelevant, doesn't matter. We can look and see that it's so wildly outside our range of what's acceptable, but we just don't have to pay attention to it. We pause them and that's it. We can do the same on the other end too. $147, there's nothing more we need to do here. It's not worth our time to pay attention to this. This is making customers for so inexpensively, and again, it's branded, so that's part of it. There's nothing to do here other than pat ourselves in the back and say, that's producing a certain number of customers and that's great. Yes, we want to keep an eye on it, but we just know this one is working incredibly well. We also know that this one is working incredibly well. This would be one where I might look to see, is there any more I could get from this? Could I increase my conversion rate a little bit? Maybe. Could I fill in the blank? What could I do to get a little more out of this one? But I have to calculate the time involved. There are only 586 searches. This is a month and a half period of time. There's not a lot of search volume. I'm already getting almost all of the traffic because I'm getting 92% of the search traffic. There really isn't more to do here. Again, this is an opportunity to pat myself on the back and say, this is working. The two examples here where I would use the metrics more would be the first one and then this one. This is one that's close to 800. If I could make this a little bit better, then I might have an opportunity to get it closer to 800 and there's a lot of search volume. This would be a place where I would use the other metrics and I would say, okay, the ad relevance is above average, the landing page is above average, the quality score is good, search impressions share, there's a little more, it's even more, I could get there. Conversion rate isn't great. It's okay, but it's not great. I might look to see how could I improve the conversion rate of that landing page specifically for this traffic. What do I think this traffic is looking for? Maybe it's an opportunity to create another landing page that speaks specifically to this traffic and then send this traffic to that page to have an opportunity to bring this $938 cost per conversion down to 800. Same exercise for this one, except for this one, I probably wouldn't spend the time. Why? 182 searches, I'm getting 95% of the traffic already. There really isn't a lot of there there. This one has opportunity. This one really doesn't. If I have to choose, what do I do next? I choose to work on this one because it's close. I think I can get it there. If I get it there, the payoff is going to be significant. This is the highest number of conversions. I'm close to being able to bring that down. If I make this work, I'm going to see a real contribution to my business because it will produce a significant number of conversions at that target price point. That's a very quick look at a micro level of detail to talk about this point, which is we cannot look at any of these metrics by themselves. I mentioned in the module, people ask me all the time, is such and such good? Well, is a nine quality score good? It's amazing. Is a five quality score good? It's not very good, but is a five quality score that produces customers for $558 when your target cost per acquisition is $800 good? It's absolutely wonderful. This data exists in a context. The moment we take it out of the context or the moment we look at any of these numbers in isolation, we have completely missed the point of using metrics to optimize performance.
